question: Why is availability of high-assay low-enriched uranium (HALEU) considered a critical deployment risk for advanced SMRs?

findings: HALEU is not yet widely available commercially; only Russia and China have the infrastructure to produce HALEU at scale, and a US ban on Russian uranium imports enacted in 2024 has further constrained supply options.
Approximately two-thirds of SMR designs currently in development require HALEU fuel, making its availability critical to the entire advanced reactor pipeline.

A 'chicken and egg' problem — where private fuel-cycle companies cannot invest without assured long-term commercial demand and reactor developers cannot commit without an assured fuel supply — has stalled HALEU infrastructure build-out and threatens to delay SMR deployment.

There are currently no approved transport casks that would permit economical movement of HALEU in the quantities needed to sustain a commercial fuel cycle, creating a logistical barrier to deployment.

HALEU requires a higher physical security classification (Category II) than conventional reactor fuel (Category III), meaning enrichment plants, fuel fabrication facilities, and transport operations will face significantly more stringent and costly security requirements.
